entry.title: Diagnostic Tool (Employee Data Changes)
entry.content:
You can also check 🔗️Link to the Article Diagnostic Tool (Workflow) :  Diagnostic Tool (Workflow)

🔍What is a Diagnostic Tool: It is a dedicated troubleshooting tool for issues with HRIS elements for both workflow approvals and data transactions happened through “Workflows” & “Centralised services”, that triggered either Manager Self Service (or) Employee Self Service” for both ON SAVE and On Change event types. Instead of getting lost in the Rule Trace excel execution logs cell by cell from its execution logs, this tool enables the admins to have the upper hand by having an enabled view of Snapshots on employee data changes and the business rules which are all triggered and which are all involved on the data changes happened in the system and with it’s corresponding cascading changes (dependent HRIS fields) happened in the other areas of the systems of current and future dated records. Data from the Trace would be retained only for 10 days in the Diagnostic tool, then purged after 10 days.

Prerequisite RBP enablement required to successfully access the Diagnostic tool and it’s allied features.

You have these permissions:

1 🚩

  • Administrator > Diagnostic Tool >Diagnostics Tracing Configuration
  • Administrator >Diagnostic Tool >Workflows Diagnostics Data
  • Administrator >Diagnostic Tool >Centralised Services Diagnostics Data
  • Administrator >Admin Centre Permissions >Access Diagnostic Tool

2 🚩

Now you have to configure the Diagnostics Trace Configuration: diagnosticTrace object, and its details are defined in Manage Data, and it will be actively spanned for 15 minutes to capture the data changes that happened through Centralised services.

Type of transaction supported for the data trace:

 1️⃣Workflows    2️⃣Employee Data Changes

Let’s simulate the transaction in the Diagnostic tool ->Employee Data Changes”.

As a prerequisite, you need to enable / setup the “Diagnostic Trace Configuration” in the Manage data (as you do it for Rule Trace 🔍 ). While setting up the trace object, the following are important attributes you can define and narrow down your search details based on your business scenarios. You can set the following attributes –

⭐Prime Attribute – Set the trace config status to Active

👉Start Date:

👉Requested By & For: The system start to perform the active trace for the employee data changes by the defined attributes of “Requested By” & “Requested For” in the diagnostic trace config object. If you forget to define this information, the system will not initiate the data trace.

Business Action: This field is non-mandatory. If you want, you can also skip this field, also you see this field column in your diagnostic search result as well. No need to have big thinking 😎, it gives you the course of action info, what user action made the system to trigger the source data changes either with the values either (MSS (or) ESS (or) Import, etc)

👉Additional Info:

*Supported Business Rule Scenarios: ON SAVE & ON CHANGE [HRIS Elements]

*Track centralised services for imports & API’s if the record is less than 10.

*Diagnostic tool captures execution details of ON SAVE rules during data imports up to  10 records of each import.

[Employee Data Changes] transaction trace details are always presented by two unique views (Purposely).  Snapshot view - Foundation of Data Changes / Rule Execution View - Business Rule is the foundation of the data, what are all involved in the data changes.

1 Snapshot View : Tabular record details for each stage of a save transaction. It showcases the entity that underwent the data changes by the user action either through (ONSAVE / ON CHANGE) event rules. It holds the HRIS field values, which got updated with features of striking out the old values, and colour code (identifier) of the newly added values. Green code of Added / Update. RED coded of Deleted values.

Snapshot data is nested with the transaction stages for an overview of data, so that you will have a clear understanding of the data transaction happened in the system

1️⃣Data Changes (1): Shows the record of which HRIS entity field underwent the value change by user. The number indicates the number of HRIS entities that were originally changed by the user and the corresponding changes performed by the dedicated business rule in place (Numeric count provides additional information on HRIS entities that underwent value corrections).

️Simulated Example: User has performed the changes only on the HRIS field – Location. (1- Job Info - Location)

2️⃣Related Changes (1): Shows the other field (or) data changes due to the source field value changes (originally changed as part of the data changes through ONSAVE (or) ONCHANGE events).

3️⃣Rules(4): Shows the details of the business rules executed with the number of records within the entity (or) with cross-entity as well. The Snapshot View doesn't show rule names. You can navigate to Rule Execution View to know about the rule details, which are all executed. Only changes triggered by onSave rules and onChange rules are traceable. A navigation button to “Rule Execution View” is provided within this section.

Simulated Example: Here, I have used a cross-entity rule, where a change of Location in Job Information will update the expected business values of the Pay Group field in the Comp Info category of the user profile.

💡- Snapshot view provides only the field-level information changes on the source field and other cascading fields through (ONSAVE / ONCHANGE) business rules.

Rules (1-Job Info Record): Number of rules executed and performed the data and cascading data changes on its respective HRIS entities. In this example, the change of location updated the Event Reason via an event derivation rule in Job Information.

Rule (1 – Comp Info): Cross-entity rule updated the comp field Pay Group

Rule (2  Pay Comp Recurring): Existing 2 pay components have moved to the new effective date from the previous stack records. Where you can see the updated event date.

4️⃣End-Date Correction (8):  This stage shows the changes to the end date field values relative to the record being updated or added. Use this to check if there's an end date issue during the transactions. Highly focused on the End Date Value.

Shows the end date correction happened on the entity level; the current existing active record gets delimited, and a new effective-dated record has been inserted.

5️⃣Forward Propagation (8) : Able to see, what are all the fields and it’s values that has been forward propagated as part of the data correction/update.

2.Rule Execution View : Supports both event types of  OnSave & OnChange rules, which trigger the employee data changes.

Rule Execution View provides details on the rules which triggered and performed the data update / Creation / Deletion in both Source and  Target HRIS entities.

You can able to see the quick Snapshot details in the rule execution view, and also you can able to navigate to the rule definition (Config Buss Rules) from embedded button provided.

My Other Published Links:

1.Read Audit Report Configuration

2. Creation of Deep Links in SuccessFactors Suites

 

🙏Thank You

Thank you for taking the time to read this article.

I have invested significant time and effort in preparing this content with the goal of helping SAP SuccessFactors professionals better understand and utilize the Diagnostic Tool. My intention has been to present the information in a concise and easy-to-understand manner, supported by practical scenarios and straightforward explanations.

entry.title: Cloud Integration- Activating Enterprise Messaging Capabilities for JMS Queue - CF
entry.content:
When JMS-based integration flows fail to deploy on SAP Integration Suite (Cloud Foundry) with this error, it usually points to one thing: the Message Queue capability isn't activated on the tenant.

In my current project IS got upgraded from Basic/Starter edition to Standard edition.

Recently we face an issue when I try to deploy the flow it’s getting failed to deploy.

The Error

[CONTENT][CONTENT_DEPLOY][ERROR_MISSING_SERVICE_KEY] : Unable to connect to enterprise messaging service. Missing Service Key. Please check if enterprise messaging subscription is active

 

Root Cause

A quick check under Monitor → Manage Stores confirms the root cause: there's no Message Queue tab at all, which means JMS/Enterprise Messaging was never activated on the tenant.

Message Queues (Enterprise Messaging) is a separate capability that must be explicitly enabled in Manage Capabilities. It is not switched on automatically — for example, an edition upgrade does not enable it by itself. Until it's active, any iFlow using the JMS adapter (or AS2/XI with JMS storage) will fail to deploy with this error.

Resolution

  1. Open Manage Capabilities from the tenant home page.
  2. Message Queues will show as not enabled.

 

  1. Click Edit.

 

  1. Enable Message Queues, then Save and OK.
  2. Message Queues becomes visible under Monitor → Manage Stores, confirming activation.

 

Redeploy the affected iFlows — the deployment now goes through without the service key error.

Note: Activating Message Queues triggers a tenant restart (a few minutes of downtime). Plan this during a maintenance window, and ensure no critical messages are in-flight, since processing can be interrupted during the restart. After activation, verify all iFlows come back up correctly and redeploy any that don't.

Key Takeaway

The error message suggests a credentials problem, but it's really a missing capability. If you hit this error, check Manage Capabilities before troubleshooting service keys or destinations.

Activating JMS is a simple, well-known step for most of us. But this error doesn't read like a JMS issue at all — it points to a service key/enterprise messaging service problem. The actual issue is JMS, just not obvious from the message. I'm writing this post to fill that gap on same search.

entry.title: Diagnostic Tool (Workflow)
entry.content:
You can also check the 🔗 Link to the Article Diagnostic Tool (Employee Data Changes) : Diagnostic Tool (Employee Data Changes)

Introduction:

What is a Diagnostic Tool: It is a dedicated troubleshooting tool for issues with HRIS elements for both workflow approvals and data transactions happened through “Workflows” & “Centralised services”, that triggered either Manager Self Service (or) Employee Self Service” for both ON S AVE and On Change event types. Instead of getting lost in the Rule Trace excel execution logs cell by cell from its execution logs, this tool enables the admins to have the upper hand by having an enabled view of Snapshots on employee data changes and the business rules which are all triggered and which are all involved on the data changes happened in the system and with it’s corresponding cascading changes (dependent HRIS fields) happened in the other areas of the systems of current and future dated records. Data from the Trace would be retained only for 10 days in the Diagnostic tool, then purged after 10 days.

Prerequisite RBP enablement required to successfully access the Diagnostic tool and it’s allied features.

Main Portion:

You need to have these role-based permissions to successfully access the diagnostic tools

1 🚩

  • Administrator  >Diagnostic Tool > Diagnostics Tracing Configuration
  • Administrator >Diagnostic Tool >Workflows Diagnostics Data
  • Administrator >Diagnostic Tool >Centralised Services Diagnostics Data
  • Administrator >Admin Centre Permissions >Access Diagnostic Tool

Type of transaction supported for the data trace:

 1️⃣Workflows    2️⃣Employee Data Changes

->Let’s simulate the first type of transaction supported in the Diagnostic tool's  ->“Workflows”.

Only RBP enablement is required for the workflow transaction. No need to set up MDF trace object for this transaction. From day 1 of RBP enablement in the tenant, all the HRIS-enabled workflows are under the radar of the diagnostic tool.

🖋️Taking an example of a very simple business rule: obtaining approval from the first-level manager for a username change.

Diagnostic Tool: Input the required details to pull the information from the system.

🧗First level of information pulled (Overview and quick brief of the details for the triggered workflow)

Further navigating to the next level of information: You can see all holistic information of the workflow status, and the relevant information on which entity the business rule has been placed and the details of workflow configuration and Workflow business rule and workflow id would be seen here (All displayed hyperlinked values; you can navigate to its detailed information directly by clicking on it)

You need to remember following features and limitations:

  • Auto approvals on your workflow config setup is also supported (if you enabled in your instance)
  • Workflows based on HRIS elements are supported and not supported for MDF objects until (Version #2605)
  • Workflow diagnostic data captures the Workflow transaction only if the approvals  are done through the Workflow Details page. Approvals are done through other way around through quick actions (or) through centralised tool "Manage Workflow Request" tool are not traceable of the workflow troubleshooting.
  • Workflows done through Sf Mobile Application are also not supported.

Take Away: When there is business requirement on amending the existing complex business rules especially with the cross-entity rules (or) new workflow configurations (or) troubleshooting the reported issues on your existing workflow approval not being received by the approver, you can simulate and troubleshoot yourself and see what is happening at the back end of the system configuration.

 

Thanks Note:

🙏Thank You

Thanks for taking your time for reading this article. Hope this article would have served additional insight on the potential troubleshooting tool called - Diagnostic Tool.

entry.title: Visualizing Business Processes in SAPUI5 using the sap.suite.ui.commons.ProcessFlow Control
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

In this blog post I'm going to explain the ProcessFlow control in SAPUI5 in a simple way, with the steps I followed while trying it out.

Overview:

So, in simple words, ProcessFlow is a control which is used to show a chain of connected documents as one single picture, instead of showing them as separate rows in a table.

For example, in most SAP scenarios one document leads to another document. A Sales Order leads to a Delivery, and the Delivery leads to an Invoice. If we show these in a normal list, all three will appear as separate rows, and the user cannot easily tell how they are connected or which one is stuck. So, by using the ProcessFlow control, we can show all three documents together in one single flow, with colors showing the status of each document.

So here I have created one small project to try this out, where I have taken a simple Order-to-Cash example - Sales Order, Delivery, and Invoice - and shown them using the ProcessFlow control.

Why I used ProcessFlow instead of building it myself

So, first I thought of building this on my own using some divs and CSS and manually giving colors based on status. But then I realized SAPUI5 already gives a standard control for this, so I did not have to build the status colors, click behaviour, or zoom behaviour on my own. Also, since it is a standard Fiori control, it looks and behaves the same as other Fiori apps, so I did not have to do any extra styling.

Project Structure 

The important files for this control are inside the webapp folder, mainly the view, controller, model, and manifest.json file, same as any normal SAPUI5 app.

processflowcontrol/ 
├── webapp/ 
│   ├── controller/ 
│   │   ├── App.controller.js 
│   │   └── View1.controller.js 
│   ├── css/ 
│   ├── i18n/ 
│   ├── model/ 
│   │   ├── data.json  
│   │   └── models.js 
│   ├── test/ 
│   ├── view/ 
│   │   ├── App.view.xml 
│   │   └── View1.view.xml 
│   ├── Component.js 
│   ├── index.html 
│   └── manifest.json 
├── package.json 
└── README.md

Step 1: Declaring the Data Model in manifest.json 

So, first we have to declare our JSON model in the manifest.json file, so that the framework will load it automatically when the app starts. We don't need to write any extra code in the controller for this.

"models": { 
 "i18n": { 
   "type": "sap.ui.model.resource.ResourceModel", 
   "settings": { 
     "bundleName": "processflowcontrol.i18n.i18n" 
   } 
 }, 
 "process": { 
   "type": "sap.ui.model.json.JSONModel", 
   "uri": "model/data.json" 
 } 
} 

Step 2: Create the JSON data (model/data.json)

So, the ProcessFlow control needs two things in the JSON file - lanes and nodes.

lanes means the stages, like Sales Order, Delivery, and Invoice. nodes means the actual documents which sit inside these stages.

{ 
 "lanes": [ 
   { "id": "order",    "label": "Sales Order", "icon": "sap-icon://sales-order",     "position": 0 }, 
   { "id": "delivery", "label": "Delivery",     "icon": "sap-icon://shipping-status", "position": 1 }, 
   { "id": "invoice",  "label": "Invoice",      "icon": "sap-icon://receipt",         "position": 2 } 
 ], 
 "nodes": [ 
   { "id": "SO-1001",  "lane": "order",    "title": "SO 1001",       "abbr": "SO", "state": "Positive", "stateText": "Completed", "children": ["DEL-2001"] }, 
   { "id": "DEL-2001", "lane": "delivery", "title": "Delivery 2001", "abbr": "DE", "state": "Positive", "stateText": "Shipped",   "children": ["INV-3001"] }, 
   { "id": "INV-3001", "lane": "invoice",  "title": "Invoice 3001",  "abbr": "IN", "state": "Critical", "stateText": "Overdue",   "children": null } 
 ] 
}

 

A few important points about the JSON data:

  • position (on lanes) — controls the left-to-right order of the lanes. I removed it once in my own testing file just to check, and the Delivery lane came before the Sales Order lane. So this field decides the order, and it's easy to forget.

  • children (on nodes) — this is what draws the arrow to the next node. SO-1001 has DEL-2001 as its child, so an arrow is drawn from SO-1001 to DEL-2001, and so on. Since children is written as an array, we can give more than one document ID here if the process branches into two paths.

  • state (on nodes) — decides the color of the node:

    • Positive → green

    • Critical → orange

    • Negative → red

    • Neutral → grey

  • stateText (on nodes) — just the small label shown under the node (like "Completed" or "Overdue").

Step 3: Bind the control in the view (View1.view.xml)

<mvc:View controllerName="processflowcontrol.controller.View1"
    xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc"
    xmlns="sap.m"
    xmlns:n="sap.suite.ui.commons">
    <Page id="page" title="{i18n>title}">
        <Panel headerText="Order Fulfillment Status">
            <n:ProcessFlow id="orderProcessFlow" scrollable="false" nodes="{process>/nodes}" lanes="{process>/lanes}" nodePress="onNodePressed">
                <n:nodes>
                    <n:ProcessFlowNode laneId="{process>lane}" nodeId="{process>id}" title="{process>title}" titleAbbreviation="{process>abbr}" children="{process>children}" state="{process>state}" stateText="{process>stateText}" highlighted="{process>highlighted}" />
                </n:nodes>
                <n:lanes>
                    <n:ProcessFlowLaneHeader laneId="{process>id}" text="{process>label}" iconsrc="{process>icon}" position="{process>position}" />
                </n:lanes>
            </n:ProcessFlow>
        </Panel>
    </Page>
</mvc:View>

So here we are simply binding the nodes and lanes arrays from our JSON model to the ProcessFlow control, and inside that giving the aggregation binding for ProcessFlowNode and ProcessFlowLaneHeader.

Step 4: Handling Node Clicks (View1.controller.js) 

sap.ui.define([
    "sap/ui/core/mvc/Controller",
    "sap/m/MessageToast"
], (Controller, MessageToast) => {
    "use strict";
    return Controller.extend("processflowcontrol.controller.View1", {
        onInit() {
        },
        onNodePressed: function (oEvent) {
            var oNode = oEvent.getSource();
            var sTitle = oNode.getTitle();
            MessageToast.show("Node selected: " + sTitle);
        }
    });
});

So, in the above code, whenever a user clicks on any node, the onNodePressed function will be called, and we get the clicked node using oEvent.getSource(). From there we can read its title, and any other property we need. I was a little confused initially whether to read the node details from getSource() or from the event parameters, but getSource() gave me the correct node object, so I used that.

Here I have just shown a MessageToast with the node title when clicked, but in an actual application, this is the place where we can navigate to the object page of that particular document.

Output

Conclusion:

So, this is how we can use the ProcessFlow control in SAPUI5 to show a chain of connected documents in a single, easy-to-understand picture. Instead of showing Sales Order, Delivery, and Invoice as separate rows in a table, we can show them together as one flow, with colors telling us the status of each document at a glance.We just need to give the control two things - lanes (the stages) and nodes (the documents) - in a simple JSON format, and the control takes care of the rest: drawing the lanes in order, placing the nodes correctly, drawing the arrows between them, coloring them based on status, and also giving us zoom and click functionality without any extra code.This same JSON structure is not limited to Sales Order-Delivery-Invoice - we can reuse it for any multi-step process, like a support ticket lifecycle, a purchase approval chain, or an onboarding process, just by changing the lane and node data.So this is a simple and quick way to build a document flow screen without writing our own timeline UI from scratch.

entry.title: Re: DMEEX- PL_ELIXIR-O Elixir format for split payment we...
entry.content:
Hi,

Please check note 2682253 about the available formats for PL in S/4Hana
->
Poland PL_CGI_XML_CT ISO20022 Credit Transfer Poland PL_CGI_XML_CT
PL_CGI_XML_DD ISO20022 Direct Debit Poland PL_CGI_XML_DD
PL_ELIXIR-O Elixir-O PL_ELIXIR-O

The standard logic (given you have implemented all to CGI notes),  REF13 is filling with VAT split string. This is the same string as the one used in Elixir, mapped this into Remittance Information -> Unstructured
For the <Tax> element, Tax/Rcrd/Tp = "WHT" is used in CGI only for Withholding tax 
Refer to the class CL_IDFI_CGI_CALL05_PL to understand how the VAT split data are retrieving

Best regards,
Mónika

entry.title: Re: The SAP Ecosystem
entry.content:
Hi 
For a topic related to the broader SAP ecosystem, you may receive more relevant responses in the SAP Community's such as 

  • Coffee Corner 
    there you can join discussions and publish blog posts on a variety of topics around the SAP Community - not necessarily "work" related - and get to know your fellow SAP Community members 

or depending on the focus of your a product-specific forum such as 

  •  Technology Q&A 
    This is the perfect place: From SAP BTP and ABAP Cloud to SAP NetWeaver and SAP BW - Q&A  covered with answers to questions about new technology and legacy systems. There the requestor can directly exchange with experienced experts.

  • Enterprise Resource Planning Q&A: T
    This is the perfect place: Enterprise Resource Planning

    Ask questions and find answers about SAP S/4HANA, SAP S/4HANA Cloud, SAP ERP, SAP SME solutions, and Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) software in the Q&A. Share knowledge and gain insights from community members and SAP in the Blog Posts by Members and Blog Post by SAP areas.

Please note that the SAP Learning Group is intended for discussions related to SAP Learning Journeys, certification preparation, learning resources, study tips, and achieving learning goals. Questions about SAP products, technologies, or the SAP ecosystem in general are best directed to the appropriate SAP Community forums, where product experts and practitioners can provide more specialized guidance.

Best regards, 
Margit

entry.title: Re: Msg no. -M7181 Warning to Error in Reservation

Hello @anil_tripathi226f58 

For SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, you cannot directly change standard SAP system message M7181 from Warning (W) to Error (E) using traditional transactions such as OMCQ/OBA5 that are available in on-premise systems.

 

Thanks,
Sravan

entry.title: Re: ERROR AU 133 ASSET
entry.content:
Dear Customer,

please check if the asset has depreciation area 50 in transaction AS03 on tab depreciation areas.

If the asset has this depreciation area, you go to tab General in AS03 and you  note the account derterminaion.

Than you go to transaction AO90 and select the account determination and than you maintain the account which is mentioned in the error message AU133

 

BR

werner

entry.title: Re: SAC Bug: Change in Numeric point widget display is SA...
entry.content:
Hello @konakalp 

This behavior is most likely due to a recent SAC update or formatting change in the Numeric Point widget. When a measure is defined as a currency measure, SAC now displays the currency code (for example, CHF) for both the actual value and the variance.

 

Thanks,
Sravan

entry.title: Re: Unit Conversion in SAP Datasphere using T006 (LB to KG Example)
entry.content: Both approaches ultimately achieve the same conversion result. However, the SAP-delivered Unit Conversion Views provide some additional conveniences, such as standard value-help views. These allow users to select units from a dropdown list with meaningful descriptions (for example, "Kilogram" instead of just technical unit codes like "KG"). When target units are exposed through input parameters, these value-help views improve the user experience by providing a standardized and business-friendly selection list.

entry.title: Injecting Live KPI Tiles into a Fiori Elements V2 List Report Page
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

Introduction

The SAP Fiori Elements V2 List Report is a standard, highly efficient floorplan for displaying tabular data with built-in filtering, sorting, and object page navigation. However, a common business requirement is to display high-level analytical metrics — a dashboard-style KPI tile area — directly above the SmartFilterBar. Out of the box, Fiori Elements V2 does not provide a native configuration for this.

This guide covers a complete, working implementation to programmatically inject three live KPI tiles into a Fiori Elements V2 List Report header using the following:

  • sap.suite.ui.generic.template (V2 Smart Templates)
  • Northwind OData V2 service via a local proxy
  • sap.m.GenericTile with NumericContent
  • V2 Controller Extension (plain-object pattern) for safe Control Tree injection and data fetching

 

Why I Wrote This

Recently, I was tasked with adding a "dashboard" feel to a standard Fiori Elements List Report. The moment I started researching the best way to implement KPI tiles above the filter bar, I realized there wasn't a clear, end-to-end guide for V2 Smart Templates.

Most documentation either pointed towards V4 ControllerExtension patterns (which crash V2 apps), relied on Analytical List Pages (which is a different floorplan entirely), or suggested using the SmartFilterBarControlConfigurationExtension (which breaks the UI layout).

This post walks through the complete, robust flow: extending the V2 controller, safely injecting the tiles into the UI5 Control Tree (without touching the DOM), fetching aggregate counts efficiently using the standard OData model, and linking those tiles to custom interactive fragments.

 

First, Let's Understand the Concept—The Factory Car

Before jumping into code, here is how I approach customizing Fiori Elements.

Picture a Fiori Elements app as a factory-built car. It comes pre-assembled, highly optimized, and runs perfectly. If we want to add a custom hood ornament (our KPI tiles), we can't just bolt it on anywhere, it will damage the engine (the framework).

Instead of trying to force standard XML view extensions into slots that aren't built for layouts, we use JavaScript to gently place our tiles into the DynamicPageHeader right after the view renders. This keeps the factory engine untouched while giving the users the custom dashboard they want.

 

Application Overview

The setup relies on standard SAPUI5 controls injected cleanly into the generated view. Here is the folder structure I used:

project-root/
│
├── webapp/
│   ├── annotations/
│   │   └── annotation.xml
│   ├── ext/
│   │   ├── controller/
│   │   │   └── CustomTilesController.controller.js   ← Tile injection & logic
│   │   └── fragment/
│   │       ├── CustomersDialog.fragment.xml          ← Customers popup
│   │       └── ProductsDialog.fragment.xml           ← Products popup
│   ├── i18n/
│   ├── localService/mainService/
│   │   └── metadata.xml
│   ├── Component.js
│   ├── index.html
│   └── manifest.json

 

Step 1: Configuring manifest.json

To inject our custom logic, we only need a single controller extension entry. We do not need an sap.ui.viewExtensions entry because we will inject the tiles programmatically.

Add the following to the sap.ui5 -> extends -> extensions block in manifest.json:

"sap.ui5": {
  "extends": {
    "extensions": {
      "sap.ui.controllerExtensions": {
        "sap.suite.ui.generic.template.ListReport.view.ListReport": {
          "controllerName": "tilesinlistreport.ext.controller.CustomTilesController"
        }
      }
    }
  }
}

 

Step 2: The Controller Extension (Injection & Logic)

Create a controller file at webapp/ext/controller/CustomTilesController.controller.js .

This is the core of the solution. It builds the tiles, waits for the standard Fiori Elements view to render, finds the DynamicPageHeader, and injects our layout. Importantly, it uses the standard OData V2 model already attached to the view to fetch aggregate data, avoiding CORS issues and native fetch() errors.

sap.ui.define([
    "sap/m/GenericTile",
    "sap/m/TileContent",
    "sap/m/NumericContent",
    "sap/ui/layout/HorizontalLayout",
    "sap/ui/model/Filter",
    "sap/ui/model/FilterOperator",
    "sap/m/MessageToast",
    "sap/ui/core/Fragment"
], function (GenericTile, TileContent, NumericContent, HorizontalLayout,
             Filter, FilterOperator, MessageToast, Fragment) {
    "use strict";

    return {
        // Called by V2 Smart Template after the view renders
        onAfterRendering: function () {
            if (!this._bTilesInjected) {
                this._bTilesInjected = true;
                this._injectTiles();
            }
        },

        // Build and inject tile layout into the DynamicPageHeader
        _injectTiles: function () {
            var oView = this.getView();

            // Store NumericContent refs for value updates after data fetch
            this._oNumTotalOrders = new NumericContent({ value: "...", valueColor: "Good",    indicator: "None", withMargin: false });
            this._oNumProducts    = new NumericContent({ value: "...", valueColor: "Neutral", indicator: "None", withMargin: false });
            this._oNumCustomers   = new NumericContent({ value: "...", valueColor: "Neutral", indicator: "None", withMargin: false });

            var oLayout = new HorizontalLayout({
                allowWrapping: true,
                content: [
                    this._makeTile("Total Orders",    "All records",  "Orders",    this._oNumTotalOrders, this._onTotalOrdersPress.bind(this)),
                    this._makeTile("Total Products",  "In catalogue", "Products",  this._oNumProducts,    this._onProductsPress.bind(this)),
                    this._makeTile("Total Customers", "Registered",   "Customers", this._oNumCustomers,   this._onCustomersPress.bind(this))
                ]
            }).addStyleClass("sapUiSmallMarginTop sapUiSmallMarginBottom sapUiSmallMarginBeginEnd");

            // Primary strategy: Inject into DynamicPageHeader
            var aAll = oView.findAggregatedObjects(true);
            var oDynamicPage = null;
            var oSFB = null;

            for (var i = 0; i < aAll.length; i++) {
                var sName = aAll[i].getMetadata().getName();
                if (sName === "sap.f.DynamicPage") { oDynamicPage = aAll[i]; }
                if (sName === "sap.ui.comp.smartfilterbar.SmartFilterBar") { oSFB = aAll[i]; }
            }

            if (oDynamicPage && oDynamicPage.getHeader()) {
                oDynamicPage.getHeader().addContent(oLayout);
                this._fetchAllTileData();
                return;
            }

            // Fallback strategy: Inject via UI5 Control Tree (NO DOM MANIPULATION)
            if (oSFB) {
                var oParent = oSFB.getParent();
                if (oParent && typeof oParent.insertContent === "function") {
                    oParent.insertContent(oLayout, 0); // Insert above the SmartFilterBar
                    this._fetchAllTileData();
                } else if (oParent && typeof oParent.insertItem === "function") {
                    oParent.insertItem(oLayout, 0);
                    this._fetchAllTileData();
                }
            }
        },

        // GenericTile factory — MUST use tileContent aggregation in constructor
        _makeTile: function (sHeader, sSubheader, sUnit, oNumericContent, fnPress) {
            var oTile = new GenericTile({
                header: sHeader,
                subheader: sSubheader,
                frameType: "TwoByOne",
                tileContent: [
                    new TileContent({ unit: sUnit, content: oNumericContent })
                ]
            });
            oTile.addStyleClass("sapUiTinyMarginEnd");
            if (fnPress) { oTile.attachPress(fnPress); }
            return oTile;
        },

        // ── Press Handlers ────────────────────────────────────────────────

        _onTotalOrdersPress: function () {
            var oSFB = this._getSmartFilterBar();
            if (oSFB) { oSFB.clear(); oSFB.search(); }
            var sCount = this._oNumTotalOrders.getValue();
            MessageToast.show("Showing all " + sCount + " orders");
        },

        _onProductsPress: function () {
            var oView = this.getView();
            var sTitle = "Products (" + this._oNumProducts.getValue() + ")";
            if (!this._oProductsDialog) {
                Fragment.load({
                    id: oView.getId() + "-products",
                    name: "tilesinlistreport.ext.fragment.ProductsDialog",
                    controller: this
                }).then(function (oDialog) {
                    this._oProductsDialog = oDialog;
                    oView.addDependent(oDialog);
                    oDialog.setTitle(sTitle);
                    oDialog.open();
                }.bind(this));
            } else {
                this._oProductsDialog.setTitle(sTitle);
                this._oProductsDialog.open();
            }
        },

        onProductsDialogClose: function () {
            if (this._oProductsDialog) { this._oProductsDialog.close(); }
        },

        onProductSearch: function (oEvent) {
            var sQuery = oEvent.getParameter("newValue") || oEvent.getParameter("query") || "";
            var oTable = sap.ui.core.Fragment.byId(this.getView().getId() + "-products", "productsTable");
            if (oTable) {
                var oBinding = oTable.getBinding("items");
                oBinding.filter(sQuery ? [new Filter([
                    new Filter("ProductName", FilterOperator.Contains, sQuery)
                ], false)] : []);
            }
        },

        _onCustomersPress: function () {
            var oView = this.getView();
            var sTitle = "Customers (" + this._oNumCustomers.getValue() + ")";
            if (!this._oCustomersDialog) {
                Fragment.load({
                    id: oView.getId() + "-customers",
                    name: "tilesinlistreport.ext.fragment.CustomersDialog",
                    controller: this
                }).then(function (oDialog) {
                    this._oCustomersDialog = oDialog;
                    oView.addDependent(oDialog);
                    oDialog.setTitle(sTitle);
                    oDialog.open();
                }.bind(this));
            } else {
                this._oCustomersDialog.setTitle(sTitle);
                this._oCustomersDialog.open();
            }
        },

        onCustomersDialogClose: function () {
            if (this._oCustomersDialog) { this._oCustomersDialog.close(); }
        },

        onCustomerSearch: function (oEvent) {
            var sQuery = oEvent.getParameter("newValue") || oEvent.getParameter("query") || "";
            var oTable = sap.ui.core.Fragment.byId(this.getView().getId() + "-customers", "customersTable");
            if (oTable) {
                var oBinding = oTable.getBinding("items");
                oBinding.filter(sQuery ? [new Filter([
                    new Filter("CompanyName",  FilterOperator.Contains, sQuery),
                    new Filter("ContactName",  FilterOperator.Contains, sQuery),
                    new Filter("CustomerID",   FilterOperator.Contains, sQuery),
                    new Filter("City",         FilterOperator.Contains, sQuery),
                    new Filter("Country",      FilterOperator.Contains, sQuery)
                ], false)] : []);
            }
        },

        // ── Helpers & Data Fetching ───────────────────────────────────────

        _getSmartFilterBar: function () {
            var aAll = this.getView().findAggregatedObjects(true);
            for (var i = 0; i < aAll.length; i++) {
                if (aAll[i].getMetadata().getName() === "sap.ui.comp.smartfilterbar.SmartFilterBar")
                    return aAll[i];
            }
            return null;
        },

        _fetchAllTileData: function () {
            // Using the existing standard OData V2 Model attached to the view.
            var oModel = this.getView().getModel();
            var that = this;

            var fnGetCount = function (sPath) {
                return new Promise(function (resolve) {
                    oModel.read(sPath, {
                        urlParameters: { "$top": 0, "$inlinecount": "allpages" },
                        success: function (oData) {
                            var iCount = (oData && oData.__count) ? parseInt(oData.__count, 10) : 0;
                            resolve(iCount);
                        },
                        error: function () {
                            resolve(0); // Fail gracefully
                        }
                    });
                });
            };

            Promise.all([
                fnGetCount("/Orders"),
                fnGetCount("/Products"),
                fnGetCount("/Customers")
            ]).then(function (aCounts) {
                that._oNumTotalOrders.setValue(String(aCounts[0]));
                that._oNumProducts.setValue(String(aCounts[1]));
                that._oNumCustomers.setValue(String(aCounts[2]));
            });
        }
    };
});

 

Step 3: Interactive Dialog Fragments

When a user interacts with the "Total Customers" or "Total Products" tiles, a searchable dialog opens. These are built using standard XML fragments binding directly to the existing OData model.

Note that the dialog titles in the fragments below are left as plain labels ("Products", "Customers") rather than hardcoded counts—the controller sets the live count on the title at runtime (see _onProductsPress / _onCustomersPress in Step 2), so the number a user sees always matches the tile above it.

Here is the setup for the Products Dialog (webapp/ext/fragment/ProductsDialog.fragment.xml)

<core:FragmentDefinition xmlns="sap.m" xmlns:core="sap.ui.core">
    <Dialog id="productsDialog" title="Products" contentWidth="70%" contentHeight="60%" resizable="true" draggable="true">
        <content>
            <Table id="productsTable" items="{/Products}" growing="true" growingThreshold="20">
                <headerToolbar>
                    <Toolbar>
                        <Title text="All Products"/>
                        <ToolbarSpacer/>
                        <SearchField width="30%" search=".onProductSearch" liveChange=".onProductSearch"/>
                    </Toolbar>
                </headerToolbar>
                <columns>
                    <Column width="5rem"><Text text="ID"/></Column>
                    <Column><Text text="Product Name"/></Column>
                    <Column><Text text="Unit Price"/></Column>
                    <Column><Text text="Units In Stock"/></Column>
                    <Column><Text text="Units On Order"/></Column>
                    <Column><Text text="Discontinued"/></Column>
                </columns>
                <items>
                    <ColumnListItem>
                        <cells>
                            <Text text="{ProductID}"/>
                            <Text text="{ProductName}"/>
                            <ObjectNumber number="{UnitPrice}" unit="USD"/>
                            <Text text="{UnitsInStock}"/>
                            <Text text="{UnitsOnOrder}"/>
                            <Text text="{Discontinued}"/>
                        </cells>
                    </ColumnListItem>
                </items>
            </Table>
        </content>
        <beginButton>
            <Button text="Close" press=".onProductsDialogClose"/>
        </beginButton>
    </Dialog>
</core:FragmentDefinition>

 

Here is the setup for the Customers Dialog (webapp/ext/fragment/CustomersDialog.fragment.xml)

<core:FragmentDefinition xmlns="sap.m" xmlns:core="sap.ui.core">
    <Dialog id="customersDialog" title="Customers" contentWidth="70%" contentHeight="60%" resizable="true" draggable="true">
        <content>
            <Table id="customersTable" items="{/Customers}" growing="true" growingThreshold="20">
                <headerToolbar>
                    <Toolbar>
                        <Title text="All Registered Customers"/>
                        <ToolbarSpacer/>
                        <SearchField width="30%" search=".onCustomerSearch" liveChange=".onCustomerSearch"/>
                    </Toolbar>
                </headerToolbar>
                <columns>
                    <Column width="8rem"><Text text="Customer ID"/></Column>
                    <Column><Text text="Company Name"/></Column>
                    <Column><Text text="Contact Name"/></Column>
                    <Column><Text text="Contact Title"/></Column>
                    <Column><Text text="City"/></Column>
                    <Column><Text text="Country"/></Column>
                    <Column><Text text="Phone"/></Column>
                </columns>
                <items>
                    <ColumnListItem>
                        <cells>
                            <Text text="{CustomerID}"/>
                            <Text text="{CompanyName}"/>
                            <Text text="{ContactName}"/>
                            <Text text="{ContactTitle}"/>
                            <Text text="{City}"/>
                            <Text text="{Country}"/>
                            <Text text="{Phone}"/>
                        </cells>
                    </ColumnListItem>
                </items>
            </Table>
        </content>
        <beginButton>
            <Button text="Close" press=".onCustomersDialogClose"/>
        </beginButton>
    </Dialog>
</core:FragmentDefinition>

 

A Few Things to Keep in Mind

When customizing standard templates, it is incredibly easy to run into framework errors. Here are the pitfalls to watch out for:

  1. Avoid direct DOM manipulation — Never use document.createElement or parentNode.insertBefore in Fiori Elements. If the view re-renders, our DOM elements will be destroyed. Always use framework aggregations like .insertContent() or .insertItem() to safely place elements into the UI5 Control Tree.
  2. Leverage the OData model for counts — Avoid hardcoded URLs and native fetch() calls. Use oModel.read() with the URL parameters "$top": 0 and "$inlinecount": "allpages". This fetches just the count without downloading a heavy payload, and it natively handles SAP Cloud routing, BTP destinations, and security headers.
  3. Wrong extension point — Do NOT use SmartFilterBarControlConfigurationExtension for layout tiles. That slot strictly accepts sap.ui.comp.smartfilterbar.ControlConfiguration elements. If we attempt to place a layout container there, the app will throw an aggregation error.
  4. Controller pattern mismatch—V2 extensions mandate a plain object return pattern (return { ... };). If we accidentally use the V4 ControllerExtension.extend() pattern, our V2 app will crash immediately upon loading.
  5. Missing the .controller.js suffix—V2 Smart Templates automatically append .controller.js during module resolution. Naming our file CustomTilesController.js will cause a silent load failure.
  6. Using addContent() on GenericTile — GenericTile does not have an addContent() method; the required aggregation is tileContent.  Always define it in the constructor. Attempting to use .addContent() will throw a TypeError. 

 

Conclusion

This approach allows us to deliver high-visibility analytical data to our end-users without breaking core framework files or generated code, keeping our application clean, robust, and upgrade-safe. As always, test the fallback paths against our own app's layout before shipping—the patterns here are proven, but every customer's List Report has its own quirks.
